reasoning:
The EFFR has printed at exactly 4.33% on every single day since January 2025, showing zero daily variance outside of FOMC rate changes. FOMC meetings for the remainder of 2025 are scheduled for July 29-30, Sept 16-17, Oct 28-29, and Dec 9-10. 

For the first two resolution dates (June 29 and July 22), there are no scheduled FOMC meetings between the June 22 baseline and the target date. The probability of an increase rests entirely on an unscheduled emergency rate hike or an extreme liquidity breakdown that causes the EFFR to float upward independently of the target range. In the current ample-reserves regime, this is incredibly unlikely.

For the September 20 and December 19 resolution dates, there is time for the FOMC to adjust rates. However, market expectations strongly lean toward rate cuts or holds. Fed funds futures currently completely price out hikes, pricing a ~60% probability of a rate cut by September instead. The probability of the rate being higher than 4.33% is limited to the tail risk that unexpectedly hot inflation forces the Fed to resume rate hikes later in the year. The likelihood of this macro scenario steadily increases as the forecast horizon expands, pulling the probability for December higher.
